speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I put my hand to my  mouth and I was trying not to  cough, I thought I gonna die.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, cough. It's normal. You're still a human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Nice human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, Peter and I have done quite a few of these together already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. It's March the 26th, 1995. The survivor is Sam Steinberg. Interviewer Carol Stulberger-- berg, Stulberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Beverly Hills, USA, and it will be in English. There is a correction. Today's March the 27th, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Carol Stulberg in Beverly Hills, California. It's March 27th, 1995, and I have the privilege of interviewing Mr. Sam Steinberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: For?

speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Foundation.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name and spelling [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Steinberg, would you give us your name please, your full name, and spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name in-- right now in America, is Samuel Steinberg, S-T-E-I-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at home was Sychmho Steinberg, S-Y-C-H-M-- [LAUGHS] --M-H-O. Sychmho S-C-H-S-T-E-I-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born September 1st, 1928 in a city called Tomaszow Mazowiecki, which is near Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Abraham. My mother was Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's maiden?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names of your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister was Faiga and my older brother was Pesach. And I was the youngest, Sychmho.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father by profession was a glazer. He put windows and mirrors. But we also had a store of kitchen utensils, which mostly were just kitchen utensils. And that was his profession that he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell us a little bit about your life in Tomaszow as a child, what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, we're going now back 67 years and memories are sometimes difficult. My memories and recollections, I probably go back about five, six years old. We lived in an apartment building consisting probably of 30 or 40 tenants, all Jewish. The only Gentile was a caretaker. My activities was going to school in the morning, to public school. But the public school also consisted mostly of Jewish kids. And then after school, to go to cheder. And then come home and play the backyard and go to sleep and get ready for another day of school. Basically, that was my activities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was basically, to-- to-- just an idea, that when the war broke out in 1939, I was only 11 years old. So I really didn't have any social life. To be-- the town was not too huge and basically, I didn't even go to a movie in Poland. But it was a happy life. I mean, we come from a-- not a well-- economic-- rich economic life, but it was-- there was never any need of anything. I mean, the necessities were always there. I had loving parents who gave me most of the stuff that I-- I needed. And that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let me ask you, did you have grandparents living nearby as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only grandparents that I remember was my father's father, and he passed away in 1935. So my recollections of grandparents are nil, actually. I-- so as far as knowing him, that's about all I can recall of my grandfather. But from my mother's side, I did not have any-- any parents at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were holidays like in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Holidays were terrific. [LAUGHS] Holidays, I guess, even Shabbat, because my father was not an Orthodox. He was a modern Orthodox, but the activity of Shabbat is to go to shul and getting dressed and coming home for the Friday night festive dinner and  Saturday night dinner was terrific. Any other holidays, where it was just something that you looked forward to. You know? Passover, of course, that's just a matter of changing the whole routine of life. I guess a person cannot visualize over here what went through in Passover. I mean, the cleaning and the painting and the changing of [INAUDIBLE]. My parents slept on a mattress, but I and my brother slept on a straw mattress. So it meant that you would burn the straw and-- and replace the straw. And-- so it was definitely a festive activity. It was things that we looked forward for holidays. It was just a-- a change of routine life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what's the most vivid memory you have of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The vivid memory of my mother was the hard work that she put in. She had to take care of a household. And we lived basically in a three-room apartment consisting of a kitchen, which were the kitchen and the, like, eating room during the-- the weekdays. The next room was a dining room and a bedroom consisting of the main bed for my parents and-- and another bed for my two brothers. And there was another room that my sister had, her own room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to take care of the household, to bake and cook. You didn't go out shopping like you do over here, I mean, for necessities, anyway. And still helped my father in the store. So her life was full. Never heard her complain. I have never heard arguments in my house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the idea of-- of a strong lady taking care of a family of five. Did you have Gentile friends as well as a lot of Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. Never had any Gentile friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived basically in a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in a Jewish neighborhood, mingled with Jewish kids in-- in the public school and cheder. So my contact with Gentiles would be during because, as I say, we had the store. And on Friday, we would have a market day, which means we would take the wares out of the store into the market where the farmers would come and buy. So this is basically the context of Gentile people that I will have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experienced any anti-semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally not. I mean, a kid throwing a stone and say Jew. Nothing specific. Nothing because, as I say, 90% of it was between the Jewish people, so my specific contact with Gentile was not there. But it was there and I heard about it and I heard from my parents, but I personally did not have the contact of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your first-- what do you remember as being the first signs of real trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the real trouble, of course, was 1939. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- no, I guess it was, again, going back about '37, when an influx of German Jews were coming in. They were being sent out of Germany. And again, it is just [? vaguely ?] situation. I was just not aware on a [? contact ?] [? deal, ?] but I heard what my parents were saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard your parents talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: And talk about it, that the people were going out from Germany. And there was still nothing to be getting scared of, I guess. We were not-- nothing specific about it. Of course, the next phase was the war of September 1939, where I think the first time in my life, I saw a plane flying through over the city. We were not bombed specifically at that-- at the beginning. And then, of course, we heard the war broke out, and we heard that England declared war against Germany. And we-- there was a utopia feeling, this super country is going to come in, and there won't be much to worry about. And-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] but then what happened is that in a few days, they did start bombing some of Tomaszow. And we decided to pack our belongings and start to run away, you know, hoping to get away from the-- from the incoming Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had no car. We had no buggies. So what you did is you-- you took your belongings on your back. And [INAUDIBLE] [? you say, ?] what do you take from-- from a home like ours? Linen and-- and some silverware and stuff like this that you take along.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that night is vivid, because it is pitch dark. And you walk on the streets with hundreds of other people and you see this wall of flame that you basically walked into. It really was not on the street. It was on the side. But when you walked towards it, it looked like a wall of flame. And after a few hours, your packages became heavy and you would throw them away and less and less. And after one night walking, I don't know how-- how many hours we walked on the [? deal, ?] we realized how futile it was. And the Germans were already coming in. So you walked back to home. And walking back, a boy of 11, seeing the dead Polish soldiers in the ditches, the horses in the ditches, left over armament-- I mean, a armament was consisted of a-- of a field gun. Those are my beginning of my vivid beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got back to Tomaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We back-- went back to our apartment, started to-- to go on a [? routiney ?] life. There was no school. The school was automatically-- I did not go back to school. So in September, I just-- in the summer, I finished five years of public school, and that was my amount of schooling that I had. Again, maybe I'll put one step forward. Sometimes people ask me, how did you survive? And throughout my story, the only thing I can say is luck. My father, being a glazer, has worked for government job people, for-- for the city or-- in Tomaszow, for certain things. When the Germans came in, they took over factories and factories had all dark windows. So they broke out all the windows and replaced them with new ones. And I became, at 11, a worker. I came with-- went with my dad and my brother to work. So we had the necessity of life. We had the food, and we went to work. And basically, life was peaceful, [? as far. ?] We were not-- in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that last?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1940. I think it was December 1940 where we were all closed into a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tomaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomaszow, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to leave your apartment to go to this--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to leave my apartment to go into a different area. And it was from three room apartment, we went into a one room-- just a one room for everybody, temporarily. Then we only had a half a room. Again, by my father working and we were working, we were still being able to provide the necessities. We had food. That was the routine. You would get up in the morning. You-- you went to work. The-- the ghetto was run by Jewish-- like, Jewish government, consisting of Jews. They had a Jewish police which were in-- in the ghetto. Outside the ghetto, of course, it was closed and the Germans were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto enclosed by walls, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was just basically a area of-- o neighborhoods and the way buildings were built in Tomaszow which consisted of, like, a courthouse, or one house near the other. So you almost had-- the doors were locked, so you couldn't go out towards the street that you usually went. You had to go through the back and through the-- through the ghetto part [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, shall I basically continue on [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: So till 1942, that was basically the life that we have-- we have continued.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the food come from and so on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you had-- you got money. You got some-- we got from Germans who would bring out-- we would go out from the ghetto. We would walk out, because we did not work in the ghetto, we would go out. You know, a group of painters and glass men. And-- and a German who walked us to the workplace and then he would pick us up and bring us up at night. Like any ghetto, you know, as long as the people and [INAUDIBLE] maybe Jewish, everybody managed. If you had-- you sold something for somebody else and then somebody else sold it to the Polack outside, and he brought in some food. So there was that a method of coexisting. You-- you gave away your precious things that you had before to get a piece of bread and butter to exist. You know. And I believe it was around December 1942-- no, it was maybe a September 1942-- that we found out that there's going to be a-- [INAUDIBLE] send out the people out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I'll back up. During the ghetto times, of course, there were certain situations that going to bed, all of a sudden you were woke up by gunshots. You heard shootings. And you would-- what would you do? You would huddle up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you got up in the morning and again, like in '39, you would see people dead on the streets. And those were, I guess, the first casualties of intellects, of people who did something wrong or just were on the list of the Germans who were shot on this particular night. Also, I guess it was an unlucky and lucky situation for me, that in 1942, I got sick of typhus, still in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Didn't go to a hospital. I'm talking about my mom. Her love, her ingenuity, was able to cure me out of typhus. And I think that indirectly, saved my life too. But telling the later story. Anyway, in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1943, I believe, I can't-- yeah, it's '43 when the ghetto was sent out-- and the way we found out that they did not let us go like we were going out in the morning to work and then-- they did not let us go to-- back home at night. They told us that that's what going to happen, that they're going to send out from the ghetto. But not to worry, that all the family members of the workers will be put in a special place and they would be-- gonna be saved. And at that time, I believe there were about 7,000 people sent out. The following morning, I guess to prove to us, they send about four people or six people into the ghetto with a German. And I, this-- what were I then-- 14-year-old kid, 12, 13, 14-year-old kid, walked in this ghetto. It was like a morgue. You didn't see a person in the street. All you saw was the Ukrainians because the Ukrainians did the dirty work over there in the brown uniforms throughout the city. We walked to the place where I saw my mom's sister who-- with some other people from the other working people who were there. I assured them that what the German told us, that not to worry that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they'll be saved and after that, we'll be all together. And as we walked back to our working place, still I can remember I had an uncle who was a photographer and an artist, who it happens that the picture that you're going to see, he's the one who made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just happened to have a little daughter, three years old, saw me and she says, [INAUDIBLE]. I have two Leicas. You know, Leica was the best camera then. I'll give them to you. Can you save me? Here, that little boy me. Well anyway, things like this stay in memory. Why-- why do you think he asked you that? Why do you think he thought you might--

speaker: interviewee
text: He saw me with a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: He thought maybe you could help him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He saw me-- nobody else could walk on the street. He saw me walking in the street with a German and he felt that I had some influence or some power to do it. It's just like anybody picking in the straw. If he would think logically, he would know, but that-- that was his only straw in his [? head. ?] I came back and told my dad and brother what I saw and what was involved. [SIGHS] They didn't let us back for the next three days. Then we found out that three days later, the rest of the people were sent out, that including my mother and sister. And they went to Treblinka and that's where I lost them. [SIGHS] After everybody was sent out, we were sent back to the ghetto. And I guess from about 14,000, 15,000 people, we were just about 1,000 people left, only the people who worked. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And again, necessities of our life-- like in any tragedy, life has to go on. We-- we continued to work and live there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the end of '40-- '43, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were-- the ghetto and Tomaszow was closed completely. We were sent out to a working camp called Blizyn. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In Blizyn, again, the-- we started feeling the-- the-- I mean, in ghetto, the difficulties were getting bad already at the tail end. You remember, the first time, a kosher home, my mom somehow got a hold of some horse meat and tried to cook it and found out it wasn't that terrific. So-- but it still is was liveable. In the camp, you were started out with two rations a day. You got some soup at lunchtime and some bread at night, and this was your daily necessities. Again, I and my brother went to work in a knitting factory. We made stockings for soldiers, you know, under the supervision of a German kapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you before you go further, how did you come from the ghetto to Blizyn? How did they bring you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Actually, I really don't recall, and most likely we went by train. I-- I am not specific, but we went by train, most likely. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like at Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, in Blizyn you lived already in barracks. You had a barrack and you had bunkers and you slept one alongside the other, like sardines. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As I say, you-- you would get up in the morning and go to work and then you had [SIGHS] a count, you know, to see that everybody was there. We were surrounded by barbed wires with towers under. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The police were still the Jewish police from Tomaszow were still like the kapo, and they're taking care of the-- of the daily supposedly. They watched that you stayed in line to get your food and the necessities. We did not have the-- the German. The only German was-- is in the factory, where he watched us work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the conditions? My brother got sick of typhus in-- in-- in Blizyn. My father died in Blizyn. How?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- as far as I know, he died of natural causes. Was it malnutrition? Did he give us the food? Was it broken heart? Was it a heart attack? I don't know. He died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They buried him-- it was surrounded by forest, the camp. They buried him in the forest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had the privilege to go out and bury him. But until now, 52, 53 years, I couldn't go. I became hysterical and whoever tried to convince me, I just didn't go. My brother went, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did not come. [SIGHS] That was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] basically life in Blizyn. Then I was left only with my brother and myself. My brother was very sick. He broke out in-- first of all, he lost all his weight. He was just skin and bones. He [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] broke out in terrible boils on his body. And there was no cure. There was nothing. There was no hospital. There was nothing that was involved. And we just existed on a daily basis like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was till about end of '43, I think, around December, where we were sent out-- Blizyn was closed out and we were sent out to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your brother together?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- myself and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went by train to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. A train to-- to Auschwitz. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first thing you remember is my tattoo. My name went away and my tattoo became 1840.

speaker: interviewer
text: Repeat it again, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my name disappeared and I was become B1840. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] again, I guess, it is for some reason a lark. In [SIGHS] Auschwitz, they put me into the kitchen to peel potatoes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was-- by being the kitchen, I had enough food. And I tried-- the only food I could bring out was raw potatoes. I would bring some out and give it to my brother. But how-- you eat raw potatoes in the condition that his, it was even worse to help him out. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is the basic life, again, in-- in Auschwitz. In Auschwitz, you are only three months. OK. Stop right there one second.
